system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:34 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

    41|- **Attorney involvement is episodic, not continuous**: The attorney checks in periodically and makes directional decisions, but daily work is paralegal-driven

    73|1. `lien-management-health-insurance` — Added VA cross-reference and government vs. private lien distinction
    74|2. `medical-records-collection` — Added VA records evidence (12+ month timeline, incomplete records, client self-report gap)

    80|
    81|### Key Patterns Identified
    82|- **VA liens are fundamentally different from private health insurance liens**: Federal legal authority, OGC Revenue Law Group as counterpart, GCL case numbers, months-long timelines
    83|- **VA records collection creates the worst bottleneck observed**: 12-18 months from request to receipt; records arrive incomplete; client must supplement
    84|- **PIP can pay VA liens directly**: A useful strategy to resolve the VA lien early using PIP benefits
    85|- **Adjuster reassignment can be an opportunity**: New "closer" adjusters are proactive and motivated to settle
    86|- **Unrelated treatment attribution is a variant of the treatment gap defense**: Adjuster does not deny treatment but attributes it to a different injury
    87|- **Client communication failures escalate dramatically in long cases**: 2+ year cases with VA delays produce the most extreme client frustration
    88|- **Circular dependency trap**: VA wants treatment dates to reopen case; firm needs records to identify dates — break with client MyHealtheVet access
    89|- **Attorney must eventually make the call to proceed with imperfect information**: Waiting for perfect VA records can extend cases indefinitely

    93|- Medicare/Medicaid conditional payment liens: no cases observed

    95|- Long-case client retention: how to prevent clients from accepting any offer out of frustration

   118|2. `client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement` — How poor communication leads to premature settlement or client attrition

   121|1. `case-phase-transitions` — Added evidence of backward phase transition (Settlement -> Negotiation) and PL cases skipping demand phase; evidence_count 3->5
   122|2. `medical-records-collection` — Added evidence of certified records for litigation, provider discovery during records review; confidence low->medium, evidence_count 3->5
   123|3. `client-expectations-management` — Added evidence of vague settlement language creating confusion, and client communication needs during litigation; evidence_count 2->4
   124|4. `attorney-review-triggers` — Added evidence of attorney directing expert work in PL litigation and reversing premature phase changes; confidence low->medium, evidence_count 3->5
   125|5. `statute-of-limitations-monitoring` — Added evidence of SOL countdown in auto accident case approaching 2-year mark; evidence_count 1->2
   126|
   127|### Key Patterns Identified from New Cases
   128|- **Premises liability cases are litigation-bound**: PL cases against commercial property owners with dedicated defense counsel are much more likely to proceed to full litigation than auto accident cases
   129|- **Expert work adds months to the timeline**: FCE, IME, and vocational reports require significant scheduling coordination, and rescheduling by clients or providers is common
   130|- **Records review reveals hidden providers**: During demand preparation, reviewing records from one provider often reveals referrals to previously unknown providers, delaying the demand
   131|- **Premature "Settlement" labeling causes client confusion**: When a case is labeled "Settlement" before the client agrees, staff confusion and client frustration result
   132|- **Client frustration follows a predictable 4-stage escalation**: Polite inquiry -> urgent demand -> threats -> repeated unanswered attempts
   133|- **Backward phase transitions occur**: Cases can move from Settlement back to Negotiation when clients reject offers
   134|- **Adjusters proactively check on cases near SOL**: BI adjusters may call to follow up on demand status, especially as SOL approaches
   135|- **Litigation requires certified records**: Standard copies of medical records are insufficient for court use; must be re-requested as certified if not done initially
   136|
   137|### Gaps Partially Addressed
   138|- **Demand drafting process**: Now documented as `demand-preparation-workflow` with demand phase entry prerequisites (still need actual demand letter construction patterns)
   139|- **Litigation workflow**: Now documented as `litigation-process-patterns` from one PL case (need auto accident litigation examples for comparison)
   140|- **Negotiation counter-offers**: `negotiation-after-demand` captures the offer-rejection pattern but still lacks documented counter-offer exchanges
   141|

   143|- Settlement disbursement: Still no cases reached completed disbursement
   144|- Counter-offer exchange documentation: Detailed back-and-forth negotiation tactics
   145|- Property owner notice analysis: Need more PL cases to document notice/maintenance/surveillance patterns

   181|- **Concurrent accidents create apportionment battles**: A second accident 1 month later allows each carrier to blame the other incident
   182|- **Litigation and negotiation run in parallel**: Filing suit against one defendant does not prevent simultaneous negotiation with other carriers
   183|- **Records collection at scale is brutal**: 20+ providers across 4 states required 2 full rounds of requests spanning 18+ months
   184|- **Adjuster calls can be a settlement signal**: Repeated voicemails from an adjuster wanting to "discuss settlement" indicate readiness to resolve
   185|- **Provider name confusion causes lost records**: Billing entity names vs clinical entity names can yield "no records found" when records actually exist

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Intake and Onboarding Workflow
    18|
    19|> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Standard intake workflow observed across cases:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
    26|2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
    27|3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
    28|4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
    29|5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
    30|6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.
    31|7. **Provider Setup**: Add all medical providers to the system. Send records/billing requests.
    32|8. **Phase Change**: Case moves from File Setup to Treatment.
    33|
    34|Timeline: This entire workflow typically spans 3-7 days from initial contact.
    35|
    36|## Evidence
    37|
    38|- In Case B, the initial contact came from the client's husband who provided detailed accident information, insurance claim numbers, and adjuster contacts. The firm requested personal info (DOB, SSN, etc.), sent VineSign intake docs, and received signed docs within 3 days.
    39|- In Case C, the intake and welcome call happened on day 1 (4/25/2024). Insurance claims were set up and LORs sent the same day. Records requests went out by 4/29.
    40|- In Case A, intake was complicated by the prior firm transfer — mom signed intake docs via VineSign, but the file transfer from the prior firm took over a week.
    41|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), the BI adjuster proactively called the firm looking for the LOR the day after the accident report was filed. LORs were sent to both BI and PIP within 1 day. The provider discovery process was incremental — the client provided some providers on day 1, then additional providers were identified over subsequent calls (TN hospital, TX orthopedic, concussion specialist). This iterative provider discovery extended across weeks. A second accident (occurring one month after the first) was identified during intake, requiring separate claim setup for an NYC cab company (American Transit Insurance).
    42|
    43|## Agent Guidance
    44|
    45|1. Request all necessary information in the first communication (don't spread it across multiple contacts)
    46|2. Send VineSign intake docs within 24 hours of initial contact
    47|3. If docs are not signed within 48 hours, follow up
    48|4. Conduct the welcome call within 24 hours of signed docs
    49|5. Set up insurance claims (LORs to BI and PIP) within 48 hours of signed docs
    50|6. Identify and add all medical providers within the first week
    51|7. Log the phase change from Lead to File Setup once docs are signed
    52|8. For referral cases (husband calling for wife, etc.), get the actual client to sign the docs
    53|
    54|## Pitfalls
    55|
    56|- Slow VineSign turnaround — follow up if not signed within 48 hours
    57|- Playing phone tag with the client for days during intake (use text/email as backup)
    58|- Not capturing all insurance information (both BI and PIP carriers) in the initial contact
    59|- Not asking about all medical providers during the welcome call
    60|- Not sending LORs promptly (this delays adjuster acknowledgment and can delay everything downstream)
    61|
    62|## Related
    63|
    64|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — intake triggers the first phase transitions
    65|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — intake often involves a family member
    66|- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — transfer cases have a modified intake workflow
    67|", "total_lines": 66, "file_size": 4476, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     7|confidence: low

    10|updated: 2026-04-12

    12|  - pip-claims-management

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    19|
    20|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    25|
    26|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    27|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    28|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    29|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    30|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    31|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    32|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    37|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    38|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    39|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    40|- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
    41|- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
    42|- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
    43|- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
    44|
    45|## Agent Guidance
    46|
    47|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    48|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    49|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    50|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    51|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    52|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    53|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    54|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    55|
    56|## Pitfalls
    57|
    58|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    59|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    60|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    61|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    62|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    63|- Requesting records under a billing entity name that differs from the clinical entity name (causes "no records found" responses)
    64|- Not anticipating that multi-state providers require different request methods and response timelines
    65|
    66|## Related
    67|
    68|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
    69|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
    70|- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
    71|- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — VA and other government providers require distinct handling with much longer timelines
    72|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases in litigation require certified records
    73|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — records completion gates the demand phase
    74|", "total_lines": 73, "file_size": 6371, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     2|title: Case Phase Transitions
     3|slug: case-phase-transitions
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_3_demand, phase_4_negotiation, phase_7_litigation]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [phase-change, workflow, status, case-management, filevine, litigation-trigger]
     7|confidence: low
     8|evidence_count: 5
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - medical-records-collection
    13|  - demand-timing-strategy
    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Case Phase Transitions
    18|
    19|> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Observed phase transitions across cases:
    24|
    25|### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
    26|- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
    27|- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
    28|- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
    29|- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact
    30|
    31|### File Setup -> Treatment
    32|- **Trigger**: All providers confirmed, initial records/bills requests sent
    33|- **Prerequisites**: Insurance claims set up, LOR sent, medical providers identified
    34|- **Actions on transition**: Begin treatment monitoring, send records requests
    35|- **Timeline**: Days after file setup
    36|
    37|### Treatment -> Demand in Progress
    38|- **Trigger**: Client confirms treatment has concluded, or attorney directs
    39|- **Prerequisites**: Treatment complete, preferably all records received
    40|- **Actions on transition**: Confirm treatment status with client, request outstanding records/bills, begin demand preparation
    41|- **Timeline**: Weeks to months after treatment ends (waiting for records)
    42|
    43|### Demand in Progress -> Negotiation
    44|- **Trigger**: Demand package sent to adjuster
    45|- **Prerequisites**: All records/bills received, demand drafted and approved
    46|- **Note**: In transfer cases, may skip directly to Negotiation
    47|
    48|### Negotiation -> Litigation
    49|- **Trigger**: Inadequate offer, SOL approaching, or adjuster intransigence
    50|- **Prerequisites**: Attorney decision to file, complaint drafted
    51|- **Actions on transition**: File complaint, serve defendants, begin discovery; negotiation may continue in parallel
    52|- **Timeline**: Observed at approximately 1 year post-accident when initial offer was very low
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In Case A, the case went directly from Lead to File Setup to Negotiation (skipping Treatment and Demand) because the prior firm had already handled treatment and sent a demand.
    57|- In Case B, the transition from Treatment to Demand in Progress was triggered by the attorney noting "no activity since August" and directing staff to check in with the client. Once treatment was confirmed complete, the phase change occurred.
    58|- In Case C, the case went through Lead -> File Setup -> Treatment sequentially, with clear phase change entries in the activity log.
    59|- In Case D, the case progressed through File Setup (July 2023) -> Treatment (July 2023) -> Negotiation. However, the negotiation phase lasted over 2 years due to VA records delays, multiple adjuster reassignments, and incomplete medical documentation. The attorney eventually ordered "prepare and send the demand" in September 2025 (27 months after the accident) despite incomplete records. This illustrates that VA cases can dramatically extend the typical case timeline, and that at some point the attorney must make a judgment call to proceed with available information rather than waiting for perfection.
    60|- In Case D (auto accident, DOI 2/2023), the case followed the full lifecycle: File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Settlement -> back to Negotiation. The backwards transition from Settlement to Negotiation occurred because the client refused the settlement offer. This shows that phase transitions are not always forward-only.
    61|- In Case E (premises liability, DOI 4/2019), the case skipped the Demand phase entirely and went into litigation (complaint filed, discovery, mediation). PL cases may bypass the standard demand-negotiation flow and proceed directly to suit.
    62|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA, multiple defendants), the case transitioned from Negotiation to Litigation when the BI adjuster's first offer (~$13K for a concussion/spine case with $60K+ in treatment) was deemed inadequate. A complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year post-accident. Litigation activities (discovery, depositions, IMEs, mediation summaries) ran in parallel with continued negotiation against a second carrier (Progressive). The case remained in the "negotiation" status in Filevine despite active litigation, revealing that the system status and actual workflow can diverge.
    63|
    64|## Agent Guidance
    65|
    66|1. Track phase change prerequisites as a checklist
    67|2. Do not advance a phase until all prerequisites are met
    68|3. Log every phase change with the person who authorized it and the date
    69|4. When an attorney asks about a case, report the current phase and what's blocking the next transition
    70|5. For transfer cases, evaluate which phases were already completed by the prior firm
    71|6. The attorney should approve all phase transitions from Demand in Progress onward
    72|
    73|## Pitfalls
    74|
    75|- Advancing to Negotiation without a demand in the file (observed in Case A)
    76|- Not confirming treatment completion with the client before transitioning to Demand
    77|- Letting cases sit in Treatment for months without checking on client status
    78|- Not logging the reason for the phase change
    79|- Prematurely changing to "Settlement" before client has agreed (observed in Case D — had to reverse to Negotiation)
    80|- Staff confusion about current phase when client calls for an update
    81|
    82|## Related
    83|
    84|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records completion is a prerequisite for Demand
    85|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — timing of the phase transition to Demand matters
    86|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — certain transitions require attorney review
    87|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases have a different phase pattern (often skipping demand)
    88|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — demand phase entry requires specific prerequisites
    89|", "total_lines": 88, "file_size": 6334, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern
    18|
    19|> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The escalation follows a predictable trajectory:
    24|
    25|### Stage 1: Polite Inquiry
    26|- Client texts or calls asking for a general update
    27|- "I would like to know where we are on this case"
    28|- "Just checking in"
    29|
    30|### Stage 2: Urgent Demand
    31|- Client's tone shifts to urgency
    32|- "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    33|- Multiple calls in a single day (3+ calls on same day observed)
    34|- Voicemails become longer and more frustrated
    35|
    36|### Stage 3: Threats and Distrust
    37|- Client explicitly threatens to leave
    38|- "I am not settling for what you are"
    39|- "You keep in touch with me or nothing"
    40|- "If you will today, you just please call me back today and just give me something"
    41|- Client may call multiple staff members when the attorney is unavailable
    42|
    43|### Stage 4: Repeated Unanswered Attempts
    44|- Client calls are logged as "missed calls" repeatedly over weeks/months
    45|- Voicemail transcriptions become shorter and more resigned
    46|- Client may call different staff members (attorney, paralegal, receptionist)
    47|
    48|### Key Triggers
    49|- Long periods of no proactive contact from the firm
    50|- Settlement offer communicated without full explanation
    51|- Being told the case is "on settlement" without a timeline
    52|- Calls going unreturned or to voicemail
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In an auto accident case (negotiation phase), the client's frustration escalated over 12+ months:
    57|  - 6/2024: "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    58|  - 3/2025: Voicemail — "will you please get back in contact with me... you keep in touch with me or nothing... call me back today and just give me something. I told you I am not settling"
    59|  - 3/2025-3/2026: Multiple missed calls logged (3/21/2025 alone had 2 missed calls + 1 outbound call + texts)
    60|  - 8/2025: Multiple missed calls to different staff members
    61|  - 2/2026, 3/2026: Continued missed calls with empty voicemail transcriptions
    62|- In a premises liability case, the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out" — followed by the attorney providing a substantive update about next steps.
    63|
    64|## Agent Guidance
    65|
    66|1. Set automated 2-week check-in reminders for every active case in demand/negotiation phase
    67|2. When a client calls and you cannot provide an update, say WHEN they can expect one (e.g., "I will have an update by Friday")
    68|3. If you cannot reach the client (their voicemail is full or not set up), send a text immediately
    69|4. When a client shows Stage 2 behavior, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
    70|5. Never tell a client the case is "on settlement" unless the client has already agreed to the amount
    71|6. After every client contact, set the next follow-up date in the system
    72|7. Document all contact attempts — even missed calls — in the activity log
    73|8. When multiple missed calls from one client occur in a day, treat it as an urgent response needed
    74|
    75|## Pitfalls
    76|
    77|- Letting client calls go unreturned for days/weeks
    78|- Client voicemail being full (preventing you from leaving a message) without pivoting to text
    79|- Staff saying "I'm not sure on status" when client calls — everyone on the case should know the current status
    80|- Responding to frustration with vague reassurances instead of specific next steps
    81|- Not escalating frustrated clients to the attorney
    82|- Allowing the pattern to reach Stage 4 before taking action
    83|
    84|## Related
    85|
    86|- [[client-expectations-management]] — proactive management prevents this escalation
    87|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — negotiation phase is when frustration peaks
    88|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — frustrated clients should be escalated to attorney
    89|", "total_lines": 88, "file_size": 4591, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    14|---
    15|
    16|# Client Communication Through Family Members
    17|
    18|> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.
    19|
    20|## Pattern
    21|
    22|Two common scenarios emerge:
    23|
    24|**Spouse as Primary Contact**: The spouse handles all communication, forwards medical documents, asks status questions, and relays information to the injured client. The injured client may have signed the retainer but is less directly involved in day-to-day case management.
    25|
    26|**Parent with POA**: When a client is incarcerated or otherwise unavailable, a parent with Power of Attorney may serve as the authorized representative. The POA document must be obtained and verified before the firm can discuss case details.
    27|
    28|In both scenarios, the family member tends to:
    29|- Be more proactive about asking for updates
    30|- Forward bills and medical documents as they arrive
    31|- Express urgency about resolution ("we want this to go away")
    32|- Ask about the impact of unpaid bills on credit
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, the client's husband was the primary communicator throughout the entire case. He forwarded all medical bills, relayed appointment results, asked about case status repeatedly, and expressed concern about bills going to collections. The client herself was largely absent from communications.
    37|- In Case A, the client was incarcerated and the client's mother (with POA) was the sole point of contact. The mother provided intake information, the POA document, and followed up on case status.
    38|
    39|## Agent Guidance
    40|
    41|1. During intake, identify who the primary point of contact will be
    42|2. If it's a family member, verify authorization: POA for non-spouse, general authorization for spouse
    43|3. Document the authorized contact in the case file with their contact info
    44|4. Set expectations with the family member about case timeline and the process
    45|5. When the family member forwards bills/records, acknowledge receipt promptly and explain next steps
    46|6. When the family member expresses urgency, respond with specific status updates rather than vague reassurances
    47|7. If bills are going to collections, explain the process (PIP submission, timeline) proactively
    48|
    49|## Pitfalls
    50|
    51|- Discussing case details with a family member who has not been properly authorized
    52|- Assuming the family member is accurately relaying information to/from the client
    53|- Not setting expectations with the family member about realistic case timelines
    54|- Ignoring repeated follow-up inquiries from family members (this erodes trust)
    55|- Not having a clear protocol for who can authorize settlement decisions
    56|
    57|## Related
    58|
    59|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing expectations is crucial when family members push for faster resolution
    60|- [[intake-via-referral]] — cases often come through family referrals
    61|", "total_lines": 60, "file_size": 3426, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Litigation Process Patterns
    19|
    20|> When PI cases proceed to litigation, they enter a structured legal process involving complaint filing, discovery (interrogatories, document production), depositions, motions practice, and potential mediation or trial. Litigation dramatically increases case complexity, timeline, and administrative burden on the paralegal team.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The litigation lifecycle follows a predictable sequence:
    25|
    26|### 1. Complaint Filing
    27|- Draft complaint with interrogatories (IROG)
    28|- File with the circuit court
    29|- Check service of process on defendant
    30|- Court assigns case number and division
    31|
    32|### 2. Discovery Phase
    33|- **Interrogatories**: Written questions exchanged between parties
    34|- **Supplemental responses**: As new information becomes available, supplemental discovery responses must be filed
    35|- **Document production**: Medical records, tax returns, employment records
    36|- **Subpoenas**: Third-party records (e.g., bank records from Hometown Bank) obtained via subpoena
    37|- **Timeline**: Court sets discovery deadlines; joint motions to extend are common
    38|
    39|### 3. Motions Practice
    40|- Motions to compel (when opposing party doesn't respond to discovery)
    41|- Motions to withdraw counsel
    42|- Joint motions for deadline extensions
    43|- Motion hearings may be remote or rescheduled
    44|
    45|### 4. Mediation
    46|- Typically scheduled after discovery is substantially complete
    47|- Mediator selected through national organizations (e.g., NADN)
    48|- Both parties must agree on mediator and date
    49|- Half-day sessions are common; can be via Zoom
    50|- Mediation may be cancelled and rescheduled
    51|
    52|### 5. Expert Coordination
    53|- FCE and IME reports ordered during litigation
    54|- Expert invoices must be tracked and paid
    55|- Reports may need amendments as treatment progresses
    56|
    57|### Court Filing System
    58|- Kentucky courts use eFiling with automated NEF (Notice of Electronic Filing) and NCP notifications
    59|- Multiple attorneys and paralegals receive notifications simultaneously
    60|
    61|## Evidence
    62|
    63|- In a premises liability case in litigation (Knox Circuit Court), the litigation spanned 3+ years (2022-2025+). Activities included: complaint filing with IROG, checking service, receiving motions, joint deadline extension motions, mediation scheduling and cancellation, IME amendment requests, FCE scheduling and rescheduling, economist report ordering, deposition scheduling, subpoena responses, and supplemental discovery responses.
    64|- Motion hearings were rescheduled multiple times (from 1/20/22 to 2/14/22 via Facebook notice from clerk).
    65|- Certified records were required for litigation — regular copies insufficient. Certification forms had to be corrected (date range "04/2014 thru Current").
    66|- Mediation was scheduled through the National Academy of Distinguished Neutrals (NADN) with a retired judge mediator, coordinated with defense counsel schedules.
    67|
    68|## Agent Guidance
    69|
    70|1. Create a litigation calendar at filing with all deadlines from the court's scheduling order
    71|2. Track every court notification (NEF, NCP) and file appropriately
    72|3. Maintain a discovery checklist: what's been requested, what's been produced, what's outstanding
    73|4. For supplemental discovery responses, track what new information needs to be disclosed
    74|5. Coordinate expert scheduling well in advance — FCE and IME providers have limited availability
    75|6. When mediator sends a Zoom link or scheduling confirmation, immediately distribute to all parties
    76|7. Monitor motion hearing schedule — courts frequently reschedule
    77|8. Keep a running litigation expense tracker (expert fees, filing fees, mediator fees)
    78|
    79|## Pitfalls
    80|
    81|- Missing court deadlines (can result in sanctions or adverse rulings)
    82|- Not tracking supplemental discovery obligations
    83|- Allowing expert reports to become stale (e.g., new treatment occurred after IME)
    84|- Not verifying certified records meet litigation requirements
    85|- Failing to respond to mediator scheduling promptly (can delay case months)
    86|- Not updating the trial calendar when deadlines change
    87|
    88|## Related
    89|
    90|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases are the most common path to litigation
    91|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL is the hard deadline for filing a complaint
    92|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all litigation decisions require attorney involvement
    93|- [[medical-records-collection]] — litigation requires certified records, not just copies
    94|", "total_lines": 93, "file_size": 4768, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Shows: medical records collection (multiple rounds, multiple providers), IME coordination, prolonged treatment (injections, PT, surgery - plate + screws), client communication via text/VM, defense counsel negotiations, 3+ year timeline. WC case is a genuinely new pattern.

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Expert Reports and Evaluations
    18|
    19|> Expert reports — including Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE), Independent Medical Examinations (IME), and vocational/economic analyses — are critical for establishing damages in PI cases, especially in litigation. They provide objective documentation of physical limitations, treatment outcomes, and economic impact of injuries. These evaluations require significant scheduling coordination and expense management.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|### Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE)
    24|- Ordered when objective measurement of physical limitations is needed
    25|- Performed by specialized providers (e.g., Biokinetics through Commonwealth IME)
    26|- Client must travel to the evaluation site (may be in a different city)
    27|- Cost: ~$550+ per evaluation
    28|- Client may reschedule on their own, which delays the case
    29|- Report typically received within 2-3 weeks of evaluation
    30|
    31|### Independent Medical Examination (IME)
    32|- Expert physician reviews records and/or examines the client
    33|- May need amendments as new treatment occurs (e.g., shoulder reaches MMI)
    34|- Attorney reviews IME findings and may direct additional imaging or treatment based on results
    35|- Can be arranged through IME coordination services (e.g., Commonwealth IME)
    36|
    37|### Vocational/Economic Expert
    38|- Retained when the injury affects earning capacity
    39|- Expert (e.g., vocational economics firm) needs: medical records, employment history, tax returns
    40|- Client may not have worked due to the accident — need to document pre-accident earning capacity
    41|- Coordination involves sending expert the necessary documents and following up on report timeline
    42|
    43|### Scheduling Challenges
    44|- Expert evaluations are frequently rescheduled (provider unavailability, client conflicts)
    45|- Client must be notified promptly of schedule changes via email and USPS
    46|- Follow up on reports when they are overdue
    47|
    48|## Evidence
    49|
    50|- In a premises liability case, the following expert work was coordinated:
    51|  - FCE scheduled with Biokinetics through Commonwealth IME; originally scheduled for 6/27/2022, rescheduled by provider to 5/9/2022 (physician out of town), then rescheduled again by client to 7/25/2022 (client wanted to wait for additional testing). FCE eventually completed, report received ~2.5 weeks later.
    52|  - IME by Dr. Nazar required amendment when the client's shoulder reached MMI; new visit summary sent to Commonwealth IME for amendment.
    53|  - Attorney directed neurosurgery consultation and new MRI imaging based on IME findings — client scheduled 3 scans.
    54|  - Economist report requested from vocational economics firm; required tax documentation for 2016, 2018, and 2020 — client did not work in 2020 due to the accident.
    55|  - Expert invoices tracked through FreshBooks (e.g., Biokinetics invoice for FCE).
    56|
    57|## Agent Guidance
    58|
    59|1. When the attorney orders an expert evaluation, confirm the provider, date, location, and cost immediately
    60|2. Send the client notification via email AND USPS mail (especially for rural clients without reliable email)
    61|3. If the expert provider reschedules, immediately update the client and internal calendar
    62|4. Instruct clients NOT to reschedule expert evaluations on their own without contacting the firm first
    63|5. After the evaluation, set a 14-day follow-up task for the report
    64|6. When the report is received, route to the attorney for review
    65|7. Track all expert expenses on the case expense log with due dates for payment
    66|8. For vocational/economic experts, prepare a package of: medical records, employment history, tax returns, and a summary of the client's work limitations
    67|
    68|## Pitfalls
    69|
    70|- Client rescheduling expert evaluations without firm knowledge (delays case by months)
    71|- Not following up on overdue expert reports
    72|- Sending the wrong tax year or incomplete documents to experts
    73|- Not notifying the client of schedule changes via multiple channels (email alone may not reach rural clients)
    74|- Losing track of expert invoices and payment deadlines
    75|- Not requesting IME amendments when new treatment occurs
    76|
    77|## Related
    78|
    79|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — expert work is especially common in PL cases
    80|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — expert reports are key evidence in litigation
    81|- [[medical-records-collection]] — experts need complete medical records to prepare their reports
    82|", "total_lines": 81, "file_size": 4716, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Client Expectations Management
    18|
    19|> PI clients (and their family members) frequently become anxious about case timelines, especially when medical bills go to collections. Proactively managing expectations about the process, timeline, and their role prevents client dissatisfaction and premature settlement pressure.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Client/family anxiety follows a predictable escalation:
    24|
    25|1. **Early Phase (0-3 months)**: Client is patient, providing information as requested
    26|2. **Mid Phase (3-6 months)**: Client begins asking "Is there anything we need to be doing?" and "Are we close to resolving this?"
    27|3. **Late Phase (6+ months)**: Client receives collection notices and expresses urgency — "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away"
    28|4. **Critical Point**: Client may pressure for quick settlement at any amount to end the process
    29|
    30|The firm's response pattern is typically reactive: responding to inquiries with brief status updates. Proactive communication is rare in the observed data.
    31|
    32|## Evidence
    33|
    34|- In Case B, the client's husband followed up approximately monthly with increasing urgency. His communications escalated from "Just checking in" (August) to "Do you feel like we are close?" (November) to "We received a letter from a debt collection agency... We want this to go away" (November) to "When can we reach a resolution? We really want to put this to bed" (December).
    35|- In Case A, the client's mother followed up asking "he was asking if I have heard anything about his case" after months of apparent inactivity.
    36|- In Case D, the client left repeated voicemails over a 2-month period (Nov-Dec 2025) that went unanswered, escalating from "trying to find out if there has been an update" to "I have been calling every single day and still have not had a telephone call back." The client explicitly stated "it has been over 3 years" and "I just want everything settled... with whatever" — indicating willingness to accept any amount to end the process. This is the most extreme observed case of client frustration from communication failure.
    37|- In Case D (auto accident, negotiation), the client was told the case was "on settlement phase" and that they "can't disclose the amount yet because it might still change." The client then refused the settlement, creating a confusing situation where staff noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations." This demonstrates the danger of using vague settlement language without specifics.
    38|- In Case E (premises liability, litigation spanning 3+ years), the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out." The attorney responded with a substantive update about next steps (records production deadline). This case shows that even in litigation, clients need regular updates after court events.
    39|
    40|## Agent Guidance
    41|
    42|1. Set expectations at intake: explain the typical case timeline (6-18 months for most PI cases)
    43|2. Send proactive status updates at least monthly, even if the update is "waiting for records"
    44|3. When bills go to collections, explain immediately: "We have submitted this to PIP for processing. It may take up to 30 days. The collection activity will be resolved through the settlement."
    45|4. When a client asks "are we close?", give a specific answer: "We need X, Y, and Z before we can send the demand. I expect that to take approximately N weeks."
    46|5. Never leave a client inquiry unanswered for more than 24 hours
    47|6. Document client expectations and concerns in the activity log
    48|
    49|## Pitfalls
    50|
    51|- Being reactive instead of proactive with client communication
    52|- Giving vague responses like "Not at this time" without explaining what the timeline looks like
    53|- Ignoring the emotional impact of collection notices on clients
    54|- Allowing months to pass without any client contact
    55|- Not explaining the process steps that remain before resolution
    56|
    57|## Related
    58|
    59|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — many of these interactions happen through family members
    60|- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — collections are the primary driver of client urgency
    61|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — understanding the phase helps set realistic expectations
    62|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — the pattern that emerges when expectations are not managed
    63|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — negotiation phase requires especially careful expectation management
    64|", "total_lines": 63, "file_size": 4896, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

A complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year post-accident. Litigation activities (discovery, depositions, IMEs, mediation summaries) ran in parallel with continued negotiation against a second carrier (Progressive). The case remained in the "negotiation" status in Filevine despite active litigation, revealing that the system status and actual workflow can diverge.
- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), the case progressed through File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand -> Litigation in approximately 1 year. Complaint filed in Boone County (24-CI-00452). The case went through full litigation including discovery, depositions, and mediation (via Zoom). Mediation failed with last-best-final offers deemed inadequate. This confirms the pattern that filing suit and going through litigation is a common path when pre-suit resolution fails.
- In Case H (MVA, air evac), the case progressed through File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Litigation. Complaint filed in Barren County (23-CI-00659). The case involved a parallel subrogation action (KFBMIC v. client in District Court). Discovery, depositions, and mediation scheduling all followed. The case remained in "treatment" status in Filevine despite active litigation — another example of system status diverging from actual workflow.
- In Case I (MVA, disputed liability), the case moved from File Setup -> Treatment in a single day. After BI carrier accepted only 25% liability, the firm filed a complaint in Jefferson Circuit Court (~8 months post-accident). The disputed liability was the primary litigation trigger rather than low offers or SOL pressure.
- In Case F (WC case), the phase structure differed from PI: the case involved DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings including BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than standard PI phases. This case type uses WC-specific milestones (DWC forms, BRC hearings, IME scheduling through WC process) that map differently to the standard PHASE_DAG."}

Client nearly missed deposition — required multiple urgent texts ("Cecil, you good?", "Cecil you ready?", "Cecil?") before responding. Deposition completed successfully. Defense counsel served interrogatories and additional discovery.
- In Case F (WC case), litigation followed a different track — DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings with BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) scheduled telephonically.  - Certified records were required for litigation — regular copies insufficient. Certification forms had to be corrected (date range "04/2014 thru Current").
 - Mediation was scheduled through the National Academy of Distinguished Neutrals (NADN) with a retired judge mediator, coordinated with defense counsel schedules.
+- In Case G (MVA, Boone County), complaint filed ~1 year post-accident. Full litigation lifecycle including discovery exchanges with defense counsel (Richardson Law Group), deposition scheduling via Kentuckiana Reporters, and mediation via Zoom. Mediation failed — defense offered "last best & final" amounts that were inadequate. Zoom mediation required coordination with clients on technical setup (muting, call-in numbers). The case remained active post-mediation failure.
+- In Case H (MVA, Barren County), complaint filed in Barren Circuit Court (23-CI-00659). The defendant's insurer (Kentucky Farm Bureau) filed a parallel subrogation action in District Court. Depositions were scheduled via Kentuckiana Reporters. Mediation was being scheduled via the same service. Service of process required checking via alias — "Check for Service - Issue Alias is not perfected" — indicating the defendant was difficult to serve.
+- In Case I (MVA, Jefferson Circuit Court), complaint filed ~8 months post-accident (24-CI-005774) due to disputed liability (BI only accepting 25%). Discovery followed, with defense counsel reviewing dash cam video. Depositions were scheduled via Zoom for both the client and a witness (companion passenger). Client nearly missed deposition — required multiple urgent texts ("Cecil, you good?", "Cecil you ready?", "Cecil?") before responding. Deposition completed successfully. Defense counsel served interrogatories and additional discovery.
+- In Case F (WC case), litigation followed a different track — DWC (Department of Workers' Compensation) proceedings with BRC (Benefit Review Conferences) scheduled telephonically. Defense counsel (Pohl & Aubrey) exchanged correspondence about Form 106 requests and IME scheduling. This WC litigation model is distinct from civil circuit court litigation.
 - In Case F (auto accident, litigation), discovery involved interrogatories and requests for production exchanged between plaintiff and defendant. Defense counsel (Dilbeck & Myers) engaged actively in scheduling depositions. The defendant's deposition was scheduled via Zoom, with the plaintiff's deposition to follow immediately after. The firm communicated with the client via text and phone to prepare for deposition. Defense counsel's responses to production requests were tracked through email exchanges.
 - In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), a UIM complaint was filed separately against the client's own insurer (Allstate) in Fayette Circuit Court. The insurer filed an answer to the complaint. Discovery commenced with the plaintiff's first set of discovery. The UIM adjuster received a courtesy copy of the complaint. This demonstrates that UIM litigation follows a parallel track to BI claims.
 - In Case H (auto accident, litigation with stolen vehicle), the case (22-CI-002878) involved multiple defendants and defense counsel from different firms. Key litigation events included: defense counsel requesting and receiving a 60-day extension to answer the complaint while attempting to get the insurer to accept coverage; an Examination Under Oath (EUO) of the defendant regarding the stolen vehicle claim; deposition scheduling disputes where defense counsel was "adamant" about timing constraints; unilateral scheduling of depositions when counsel could not agree; and audio/video recording requests from the court. The case lasted 4+ years from DOI through ongoing litigation.

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/workers-compensation-case-patterns.md", "content": "---
title: Workers' Compensation Case Patterns
slug: workers-compensation-case-patterns
phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment, phase_7_litigation]
category: edge-cases

confidence: low
evidence_count: 1
created: 2026-04-12
